mappings.rationaleAI: Physical cosmology is the astrophysical study of the universe's origin, evolution, and large-scale structure, directly corresponding to B.5's scope of cosmology and cosmogony. It covers the Big Bang model and relativistic cosmological frameworks central to A.1.f.iv.

Chemistry is the foundational discipline whose nature, scope, and subdivisions are exactly what D maps out. It also covers the full historical trajectory of the science from alchemy through Lavoisier and quantum chemistry, aligning with A.3.

Astronomy is precisely the discipline whose nature, methods, and component subdisciplines B addresses. It also surveys the full historical arc from ancient astronomical records through 20th-century radio and space astronomy, spanning A.1.a–f.

Chemical element covers Mendeleev's periodic table (A.3.c.i), the discovery of new elements and isotopes (A.3.c.ii), and the nature of chemistry's primary subject matter — atomic number and elemental composition — directly relevant to D.1.

Universe encompasses physical cosmology from the Big Bang through large-scale structure and dark matter/energy, matching B.5 and A.1.f.iv's relativistic cosmologies and A.1.f.i's statistical studies of galaxies.

Physics maps directly onto C, which addresses the discipline's nature, component subdisciplines (mechanics, thermodynamics, quantum mechanics, etc.), and its relationship to other fields. It covers the full historical trajectory in A.2.

Gravity covers Newton's universal gravitation (A.1.c.iv, A.2.c) and Einstein's general relativistic reinterpretation as spacetime curvature, plus its central role in classical mechanics (C.2). Reconciling gravity with quantum mechanics connects to C.6.

Galileo is the archetypal figure of 17th-century astronomy (A.1.c — heliocentrism, telescope use) and physics (A.2.c — free fall, inertia, projectile motion), representing the core developments in both histories during the Scientific Revolution period.

Newton's Principia established classical mechanics and universal gravitation, directly corresponding to A.2.c (physics in the 16th–17th centuries) and A.1.c.iv (theory of universal gravitation). His laws remain the foundation of C.2's mechanics subdiscipline.

Einstein transformed 20th-century physics through special and general relativity (A.2.e.i) and early quantum theory (A.2.e.ii), while his general relativity enabled the astronomical tests and cosmological red-shift findings described in A.1.f.iii.

History of science surveys the development of physics, chemistry, and astronomy from antiquity through the 20th century, covering the full scope of A and its sub-paths on the histories of astronomy, physics, and chemistry.

Big Bang is the cosmological theory central to B.5 (cosmology and cosmogony) and specifically maps to A.1.f.iv's relativistic cosmologies and open/closed models of the universe, along with the CMB observations noted in A.1.f.

Physical chemistry is an explicit subdivision of chemistry in D.2, applying thermodynamics and quantum mechanics to chemical systems. Its treatment of reaction kinetics and equilibrium addresses D.4 (study of chemical transformations — the priority coverage path) and reflects 20th-century chemistry in A.3.d.

The Sun is the central body of solar science, fitting B.2's planetary and solar sciences component. Spacecraft and probe study of the Sun relates to A.1.f.v's major advances in solar-system astronomy.

The Moon is Earth's only natural satellite and a primary object of B.2's lunar sciences. Apollo manned missions and subsequent analysis align with A.1.f.v's manned lunar missions and unmanned space probes.

Energy is the core physical quantity linking matter and its interactions (C.1) and is central to thermodynamics, mechanics, and electromagnetic theory — all subdisciplines under C.2. It is the unifying concept across the physical sciences' scope.

Classical mechanics is the explicit first subdiscipline listed under C.2 and its historical development — Newton's laws unifying celestial and terrestrial motion — corresponds directly to A.2.c (physics in the 16th–17th centuries).
Optics is an explicit subdiscipline of physics under C.2. Its historical development through wave theory versus corpuscular theory and the search for the ether matches A.2.c and A.2.d.i.

Matter is the primary subject of C.1 (physics' concern with matter and energy), and its quantum mechanical and particle physics dimensions connect to A.2.e.iii's development of theories of atomic structure, nuclear interactions, and elementary particles.

Maxwell unified electricity, magnetism, and light into classical electromagnetism, directly corresponding to A.2.d.ii (development of theories of electricity, magnetism, and electromagnetic waves). Maxwell's equations define the electromagnetism subdiscipline in C.2.
Thermodynamics is an explicit subdiscipline under C.2. Its historical development through Carnot, Clausius, and Kelvin — laws of thermodynamics and kinetic theory — maps directly to A.2.d.iii.

The theory of relativity (special and general) is the defining development of A.2.e.i. General relativity's observational tests — Mercury's perihelion, gravitational lensing, cosmological redshift — map precisely to A.1.f.iii. Its philosophical implications connect to C.6.
Electricity is an explicit component of physics under C.2 (electricity and magnetism). Its historical development from ancient observation through 19th-century theory corresponds to A.2.d.ii.
Mercury is a Solar System planet whose geology, orbital mechanics, and spacecraft exploration (Mariner 10, MESSENGER, BepiColombo) fall squarely within B.2's planetary sciences component.

Stars are the primary subject of astrophysics (B.5), and their structure, formation, and evolution — nucleosynthesis through stellar remnants — correspond exactly to A.1.f.ii's theories of stellar structure, formation, and evolution.
Force is foundational to classical mechanics (C.2) via Newton's three laws, developed in A.2.c, and in modern physics reduces to the four fundamental interactions described in A.2.e.iii.

The electron's discovery belongs to A.2.d.iv (discovery of the electron, radioactivity, X-rays). Its quantum mechanical behavior and role in atomic and condensed-matter physics connect to A.2.e.iii and C.2.

Electromagnetic radiation encompasses the full spectrum predicted by Maxwell's equations (A.2.d.ii, C.2). Multi-wavelength astronomy — radio, X-ray, infrared, gamma-ray — is precisely what A.1.f.vi covers regarding 20th-century astronomical instrumentation.

Lavoisier is the central figure of A.3.b.i (studies of combustion, disproof of phlogiston theory) and contributed fundamentally to A.3.b.ii through isolation and identification of elements including oxygen and hydrogen.

Faraday discovered electromagnetic induction and laws of electrolysis, directly corresponding to A.2.d.ii. His electrochemical work and identification of benzene connect to A.3's chemistry history (electrochemistry developments in A.3.c.vi).

Jupiter is a major Solar System planet covered under B.2's planetary sciences, including its magnetic field, moons, and role in solar system formation — directly relevant to the study of planetary and lunar sciences.

The Milky Way is the primary example of galactic structure studied in B.5 and A.1.f.i's statistical studies of stars, nebulae, and galaxies. Its central black hole (Sagittarius A*) connects to A.1.f.vii.
Planet is the foundational subject of B.2's planetary sciences, covering the Solar System's eight planets, exoplanets, and the IAU definition distinguishing planets from dwarf planets.

Electromagnetism is an explicit subdiscipline of physics (C.2) and its unification via Maxwell's equations is the central development in A.2.d.ii. Quantum electrodynamics extends this to A.2.e.ii/iii.

The speed of light's measurement and invariance connect to A.2.d.i (wave theory, search for ether) and its role as a special relativistic postulate ties to A.2.e.i. Its measurement history exemplifies the experimental methods in C.3.

The periodic table is the direct subject of A.3.c.i (development by Mendeleev and Meyer) and organizes the elemental subject matter of D.1. Its quantum mechanical basis connects to D.2's physical chemistry subdivision.

Telescope maps exactly to A.1.c.iii (invention and use of the telescope in the 17th century) and extends through radio, X-ray, and space-based telescopes. It is a primary instrument for B.1's methods of study in astronomy.

The Scientific Revolution encompasses all three history-of-science sub-paths: heliocentrism and telescope in A.1.c, Galileo-Newton mechanics in A.2.c, and the early modern challenge to alchemy in A.3.a through the influence of the new mechanical philosophy.

Venus is a Solar System planet covered in B.2's planetary sciences, with particular relevance given that its observable phases provided historical evidence for heliocentrism (connecting also to A.1.c.i) and it is a target of active planetary exploration.

Mars is a primary target of planetary exploration in B.2, and its continuous robotic exploration since 1997 connects to A.1.f.v's discussion of unmanned space probe exploration of planets and their satellites.

Chemical substance defines the nature of chemistry's primary subject matter (D.1 — composition and properties of matter) and distinguishes chemical from physical change, directly addressing D.4 (the study of chemical transformations — the priority coverage path).

Oxygen's characterization by Lavoisier is the defining event of A.3.b.i (combustion/phlogiston) and A.3.b.ii (isolation and identification of gases and new elements). As a chemical element it belongs to D.1's subject matter.

Black holes are an explicit subject of A.1.f.vii (identification of possible black holes, pulsars, quasars) and are central to modern B.5 astrophysics, linking general relativity, gravitational waves, and galactic structure.

Galaxies are the primary objects studied in B.5 (study of stars, galaxies, and the universe) and A.1.f.i (statistical studies of stars, nebulae, and galaxies). Their morphology, dark matter, and large-scale clustering are core astrophysical topics.

Hydrogen as the first and most abundant element exemplifies D.1's concern with elemental composition and properties. Its identification and naming by Lavoisier relates to A.3.b.ii, and its spectral lines were historically significant for quantum mechanics (A.2.e.ii).

Temperature is a core physical quantity in thermodynamics (C.2), governed by the zeroth and third laws and fundamental to statistical mechanics. It applies across all physical sciences as a state variable, though its scope here is moderate.

Biochemistry is explicitly listed as a subdivision of chemistry in D.2 alongside analytical, inorganic, organic, physical, polymer, and industrial chemistry. Its inclusion here is as a moderate-overlap candidate covering this subdisciplinary path.
